The '''optative''' mood expresses wishes and desires in Aelaki. It is marked by the suffix '''-ya''' in the future tense and '''-nkya''' in the hodiernal tense.

== Overview ==

The optative is used to convey what the speaker wants or wishes for. It has two distinct uses: the standard (non-quirky) optative and the ''quirky optative''.

== Suffixes ==

{| class="wikitable"
|-
! Tense !! Suffix
|-
| Future || ''-ya''
|-
| Hodiernal || ''-nkya''
|}

== Non-Quirky Optative ==

The standard optative expresses a straightforward desire or wish about a real, achievable outcome.

: "I want to eat that cake."

This is a simple statement of desire where the speaker believes the wish can be fulfilled.

== Quirky Optative ==

The '''quirky optative''' uses pseudopronouns to express counterfactual wishes — things the speaker desires but knows to be contrary to fact. The hodiernal tense ('''-nkya''') is required for counterfactual readings.

: "I wish I had a car."

Because the speaker does not currently have a car, the counterfactual meaning requires the hodiernal form with pseudopronouns.

[[Category:evidentialities]]
